pages.raw_text:
Apparently the local laundry
trust has succeeded in absorbing
the Eastern laundry, the only union
institution of that kind in the city.
Taylor and Clason, who recently
purchased the plant from H. A.
Yerkes, have served notice on their
employees that an open shop would
be maintained after Saturday, and
the matter was formally brought
before the Central Labor union
last night.


A committee was appointed to
take the matter up and II the latest
proprietors of the Eastern laundry
maintain their open shop PROPOSE
ton it Is considered certain that
the various union will start q rival
laundry to the trust that cannot be
bought out


A number of delegates last night
offered to subscribe $50 apiece for
stock in such project. It's installa-
ton will mean that the trust will
lose all the union labor work it is
doing now.


Mr.. Taylor, who with George
Clason recently bought the Eastern
laundry, refused to discuss any
phase of the question today. He
would not deny or affirm the report
that the real purchaser was the


laundry association. Clason was
formerly employed at the Great
American laundry, of the trust
concerns.


Yerkes, who started the Eastern
laundry with the backing of labor
union generally because he signed
the scale of the recently organized
Laundry Workers union, was on
the trust blacklist AS soon as his


place was unionized he was given


more business than he could
handle, members or all unions
turning their patronage to him. His
employees worked less hours and
drew more pay than employees Of
the trust concerns, and tho place
has been a thorn in the anti-union
association ever since 1t started.


It's success meant that employees in
other laundries might presently de-


mand equal pay and hours, some
thing the trust is determined not
to permit.


But open OPPOSITION meant only
more support for the union laun-
dry, and quiet campaign was in
augurated, as union men look at
it. They claim that Taylor was


brought up here from Latah and as
ISOLATED with Clason to make the
purchase. Yerkes is reported to
have been paid $4,000 for the plant,


but it is likely that he got what
ever he asked, If the trust was the
actual purchaser.


At any rate, the old familiar
trust method of breaking up a


union by declaring for the open
shop has been followed. The East
ern employees, who are ali on the
trust blacklist for being union men,
have from time to time been In
formed that they could make their
peace with the trust and would De
permitted to work at their trade
again in this territory by deserving
the union.


The open shop movement means
that the union members will be
gradually weeded out until none
are left. They will have to leave
the city, as they are blackllsted
and thus the union movement will
be nipped in the bud.


But the trust has organized To-
Dor of the city generally to reckon


with in the process of benevolent
| assimilation" and this last move
| will merely represent the cost OF
